findings.name: Directory Traversal in File Download
findings.evidence: Response contains: root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
findings.description: The file download endpoint is vulnerable to directory traversal, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files from the server.
findings.recommendation: Validate file paths against a whitelist. Use a chroot or restrict file access to a specific directory. Remove path traversal sequences.

findings.name: SQL Injection in Login Form
findings.evidence: SQL error: You have an error in your SQL syntax near '' OR 1=1 --'
findings.description: The login form is vulnerable to SQL injection via the username parameter. An attacker can bypass authentication or extract database contents.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements for all database operations. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.

findings.name: OS Command Injection in Ping Utility
findings.evidence:
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
daemon:x:1:1:daemon:/usr/sbin
findings.description: The ping utility concatenates user input directly into an OS command. An attacker can execute arbitrary commands on the server.
findings.recommendation: Use allow-listed input validation. Never pass user input to system shell commands. Use language-native libraries instead.

findings.name: JWT Algorithm Confusion (none)
findings.evidence: HTTP 200 OK with admin data returned using alg:none JWT
findings.description: The API accepts JWTs with 'alg: none', allowing an attacker to forge tokens without any signing key.
findings.recommendation: Reject JWTs with alg=none. Enforce a specific algorithm (e.g., RS256). Validate the algorithm server-side.

findings.name: API Endpoint Missing Authentication
findings.evidence: HTTP 200 OK with user list returned without any authentication header
findings.description: The admin API endpoint returns sensitive user data without requiring authentication, exposing user PII to unauthenticated attackers.
findings.recommendation: Enforce authentication on all API endpoints. Use OAuth 2.0 / API keys. Implement rate limiting.

findings.name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
findings.evidence: HTTP 200 with different user's PII: {"name":"Jane Doe","email":"jane@corp.com"}
findings.description: Changing the user ID in the URL returns another user's profile data without authorization checks.
findings.recommendation: Implement server-side authorization checks for every resource access. Use indirect object references or UUIDs.

findings.name: Session Fixation Vulnerability
findings.evidence: Session ID unchanged after login: SESSIONID=abc123 (pre-auth) -> SESSIONID=abc123 (post-auth)
findings.description: The application does not regenerate the session identifier after successful authentication. An attacker who sets a known session ID before login can hijack the authenticated session.
findings.recommendation: Regenerate the session ID after authentication. Invalidate old sessions on login.

findings.name: Reflected XSS in Search Results
findings.evidence: Response body contains unescaped: <script>alert(1)</script>
findings.description: The search results page reflects user input without HTML encoding, enabling reflected XSS. An attacker can steal session cookies or perform actions on behalf of the victim.
findings.recommendation: HTML-encode all user-controlled output. Implement Content-Security-Policy headers. Use a templating engine with auto-escaping.

findings.name: Stored XSS in Comment Field
findings.evidence: Stored payload rendered in GET /blog/post/42 response body
findings.description: User comments are stored and rendered without sanitization, enabling persistent XSS that affects all visitors viewing the post.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize HTML input using a whitelist-based library (e.g., DOMPurify). Encode output. Deploy CSP with nonce-based script sources.

findings.name: Local File Inclusion via Template Parameter
findings.evidence: Response contains shadow file entries: root:$6$...:18000:0:99999:7:::
findings.description: The template parameter is used to include files from the filesystem without proper validation, enabling local file inclusion.
findings.recommendation: Never use user input to construct file paths. Use a whitelist of allowed templates. Disable remote file includes in PHP config.

findings.name: Blind SQL Injection in Search
findings.evidence: Response time: 5.12s (baseline: 0.15s) -- time-based blind SQLi confirmed
findings.description: The search endpoint is vulnerable to blind SQL injection. The server response was delayed by 5 seconds when a SLEEP payload was injected.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries. Implement input validation and WAF rules to block SQL meta-characters.

findings.name: JWT Secret Brute-Forced (weak key)
findings.evidence: JWT signature verified with dictionary key 'secret123' -- forged admin token accepted
findings.description: The JWT is signed with a weak HMAC secret that was cracked via dictionary attack. An attacker can forge arbitrary tokens.
findings.recommendation: Use strong randomly-generated secrets (256+ bits). Prefer asymmetric algorithms (RS256/ES256) for public APIs. Rotate keys periodically.

findings.name: SSRF via URL Parameter
findings.evidence:
Response contains: ami-id
instance-type
local-ipv4 -- AWS metadata endpoint accessed
findings.description: The application fetches user-supplied URLs server-side without validation, allowing access to internal services and cloud metadata endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Validate and whitelist allowed URL schemes and destinations. Block RFC 1918 and link-local addresses. Use a dedicated proxy with egress filtering.

findings.name: XXE Injection in XML Upload
findings.evidence: Response contains server hostname: 'prod-web-01'
findings.description: The XML parser processes external entities, allowing an attacker to read local files, perform SSRF, or cause denial of service.
findings.recommendation: Disable external entity processing in the XML parser (e.g., set XMLReader.FEAT_EXTERNAL_ENTITIES to false). Use JSON instead of XML where possible.

findings.name: Mass Assignment via API
findings.evidence: User role changed to admin in response: {"id":1001,"role":"admin"}
findings.description: The API allows updating fields like 'role' that should not be user-modifiable, enabling privilege escalation.
findings.recommendation: Use a whitelist of allowed fields for each API endpoint. Implement server-side input filtering.

findings.name: Session Cookie Missing Secure Flag
findings.evidence: Set-Cookie: SESSIONID=abc123; Path=/; HttpOnly (missing Secure flag)
findings.description: The session cookie is not marked Secure, allowing transmission over unencrypted HTTP connections where it can be intercepted.
findings.recommendation: Set the Secure flag on all session cookies. Enforce HTTPS across the entire application.

findings.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery on Password Change
findings.evidence: Password changed successfully without CSRF token in POST body or headers
findings.description: The password change form does not require a CSRF token, allowing an attacker to change a victim's password via a malicious page.
findings.recommendation: Implement CSRF tokens on all state-changing forms. Use SameSite=Strict on session cookies.

findings.name: DOM-Based XSS via URL Fragment
findings.evidence: JavaScript document.write() uses location.hash unsanitized
findings.description: Client-side JavaScript uses location.hash to render content without sanitization, enabling DOM-based XSS.
findings.recommendation: Avoid innerHTML and document.write with user input. Use textContent or a DOM sanitizer.

findings.name: Detailed Stack Trace Exposed
findings.evidence:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/app/views.py", line 42...
ValueError: invalid literal for int()
findings.description: The application returns full Python stack traces to the client, revealing internal file paths, library versions, and code structure.
findings.recommendation: Implement a generic error handler that returns a standard error message. Log detailed errors server-side only.

findings.name: Missing Content-Security-Policy Header
findings.evidence: Response headers: Server: nginx/1.18, X-Powered-By: Express -- no CSP header present
findings.description: The application does not set a Content-Security-Policy header, making it easier to exploit XSS vulnerabilities and inject malicious resources.
findings.recommendation: Implement a strict CSP: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-...'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'.

findings.name: Open Redirect via next Parameter
findings.evidence: HTTP 302 Location: https://evil.com/phish
findings.description: The login page redirects to an attacker-controlled URL via the 'next' parameter, facilitating phishing attacks.
findings.recommendation: Validate redirect URLs against a whitelist of allowed domains. Use relative paths only.

findings.name: Sensitive Data in HTML Comments
findings.evidence: <!-- TODO: remove before prod -- DB: prod-mysql.internal:3306, user=app_rw -->
findings.description: HTML comments contain internal infrastructure details including database hostnames and credentials.
findings.recommendation: Remove all developer comments from production HTML. Implement a build step to strip comments.

findings.name: Missing X-Frame-Options Header
findings.evidence: No X-Frame-Options or frame-ancestors CSP directive present
findings.description: The application can be embedded in an iframe, potentially enabling clickjacking attacks.
findings.recommendation: Set X-Frame-Options: DENY or use CSP frame-ancestors 'self'.

findings.name: Server Version Disclosure
findings.evidence:
Server: Apache/2.4.49 (Ubuntu)
X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.3
findings.description: The server exposes its software version in HTTP headers, aiding attackers in identifying known vulnerabilities.
findings.recommendation: Remove or genericize Server and X-Powered-By headers. Configure the web server to suppress version information.
